text:
The morning I decided to leave was unremarkable in every way except the decision itself. Coffee sat cooling on the counter, the news played from a speaker I never remembered turning on, and the cat watched me from the windowsill with the detached curiosity that cats reserve for human behavior they find baffling. I had been thinking about it for months, the way you think about rearranging furniture — not with urgency, but with a growing dissatisfaction that eventually becomes impossible to ignore.

Leaving a city is not the same as leaving a place. A place is geography, coordinates on a map, a dot you can point to and say I was there. A city is a relationship. It has moods and rhythms, good days and terrible ones, inside jokes you share with no one because they only make sense at three in the morning on a particular street corner in the rain. Chicago had been my city for eleven years. I knew which coffee shops had outlets near the window seats. I knew the sound the L makes when it curves around the loop — that particular metallic screaming that tourists find alarming and residents find comforting in its reliability. I knew that the lake would be gray in November and impossible in July, and that the wind was never as bad as people claimed unless you were standing on the Michigan Avenue bridge in February, in which case it was worse.

But knowing a place is not the same as belonging to it. Somewhere around year eight, I started noticing the gap between how I talked about Chicago and how I felt living in it. At dinner parties I would deliver passionate monologues about the architecture, the food scene, the stubborn resilience of a city that refused to be anything other than itself. These monologues were sincere. They were also performances. The truth was quieter and less flattering: I had become comfortable, and comfort had become a kind of paralysis.

The decision crystallized during a conversation with my sister. She asked what I would do if I could start over, and I gave her a list so specific and detailed that we both realized I had been planning this without admitting it to myself. I wanted to live somewhere smaller. I wanted to see mountains from my kitchen window. I wanted a yard, not because I had any talent for gardening, but because I wanted the option of failing at it. These were small desires, almost embarrassingly modest, but they had been accumulating quietly for years, stacking up like unread mail until the pile became impossible to step around.

So I left. Not dramatically — I gave proper notice, sold furniture on Craigslist, and endured a going-away party where people said kind things that made me feel guilty for wanting to go. The drive west took three days. I ate gas station food and listened to podcasts about topics I would immediately forget. The landscape changed gradually then all at once, flat giving way to rolling, rolling giving way to rising, and then suddenly there were mountains, just sitting there on the horizon like they had been waiting.
